calendar.matters:
Notice Of Petition To Compel Arbitration And To Stay Proceedings
Off Calendar May-31-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. MADONNA HERMAN & ATTY. BRANDON CARR OF THE BUCHALTER LAW FIRM, TEL. NO. 1-415-227-3515 APPEARING FOR THE PETITIONERS;
ATTY. JOSEPH M. BREALL OF BREALL & BREALL LLP, TEL. NO. 1-415-345-0545 APPEARING FOR THE RESPONDENT;
ATTY. THERESA J. BICHSEL, DEPT. OF INDUSTRIAL RELATIONS, TEL. NO. 1-415-486-2083, APPEARING FOR THE INTERVENOR.

RULING - Judge Kahn disclosed that Mr. Breall is the brother of one of his colleague. This does not affect his ability to be fair and partial. The matter was argued. The Court adopted its tentative ruling. PETITIONERS SUBCONTRACTING CONCEPTS CT LLC, JESUS GONZALEZ, and PEDRO LUESCH'S Petition To Compel Arbitration And To Stay Proceedings is Denied. The arbitration agreement is unconscionable and severance of the substantively unconscionable provisions is not possible because the entire agreement is permeated with unconscionability. The arbitration agreement is procedurally unconscionable. Subcontracting presented the adhesive contract in a take-it-or-leave-it manner and respondent Chafie De Melo, whose English skills are limited, was asked to sign it "on the spot." (De Melo Dec., pars. 1-7.) Paragraph 26 of the agreement refers to the American Arbitration Association, but the agreement does not clearly state what rules will govern the arbitration proceedings and Mr. De Melo was never provided a copy of the governing rules. "The level of oppression is increased when, as here, the employer not only fails to provide a copy of the governing rules, but also fails to clearly identify which rules will govern so the employee could locate and review them." (Carbajal v. CWPSC, Inc. (2016) 245 Cal.App.4th 227, 245.) The arbitration agreement is substantively unconscionable. The agreement requires the parties to share the costs of arbitration and such costs are often prohibitively expensive and pose a barrier for an alleged employee such as Mr. De Melo to vindicate his legal rights. (Armendariz v. Foundation Health Psychcare Services, Inc. (2000) 24 Cal.4th 83, 108, 110-111). The agreement not only precludes Mr. De Melo from taking advantage of the relatively inexpensive remedy of a hearing before the Labor Commissioner, it also bars representative and private attorney general claims. The arbitration agreement also bars the recovery of punitive damages, statutory penalties, equitable relief, and attorney's fees, which deprives Mr. De Melo of statutory remedies. The arbitration agreement is permeated with unconscionability and thus severance of the unconscionable terms is not possible. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Irene Tofft, CSR # 12313 =302/hk
